reasoning: There are currently no clinical trials or public health campaigns prioritizing a vaccine for Lymphocytic choriomeningitis (LCMV). LCMV is generally a rare, mild infection in humans (except for vertical transmission and in immunocompromised transplant recipients) and lacks the commercial and epidemic drivers typically required to fund multi-year human trials. Many recent papers use 'LCMV' in the context of it being a viral vector (e.g., used to deliver TB or CMV antigens) rather than being the target of the vaccine itself. Given standard vaccine development timelines (typically 10+ years without rapid prioritization), there is virtually zero chance of an approved human vaccine appearing by 2026-2029. The main chance for an affirmative resolution is if a Wikipedia editor erroneously adds 'Yes' due to confusion with LCMV-vectored vaccines, adds an experimental animal model vaccine, or engages in vandalism. Over longer horizons (2031-2036), the probability slightly increases to account for general uncertainty and standard horizon effects, though it remains securely below 10%.
